content.full_text:
State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assistance/Administration    •    Executive/Legal    •    Enforcement    •    Conflict of Interest
(916) 322 5560                         322 5901              322 6441            322 6444


December 27, 1977

Mr. Douglas A. Lewis                                                   77-12-087
Placer County Schools' Attorney
1230 High Street
Auburn, CA 95603

                                        Re: Opinion Request
                                            No. 77-038

Dear Mr. Lewis:

     Thank you for your letter to the Commission dated
October 28, 1977, requesting an opinion concerning certain
disclosure and disqualification responsibilities of a member
of the Tahoe-Truckee Unified School District. The Commission
will not issue a formal opinion concerning the matters in
question because your request does not raise substantial
issues of interpretation under the Political Reform Act
("Act"). See 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18329(b). However, I
trust the following informal advice issued pursuant to Government Code Section 83114(b)1/ will enable you and Dr. Black
to resolve the matter in question.

     Initially we discuss Dr. Black's reporting obligations, as set out in questions one through three of your
letter. Paramount to any discussion of Dr. Black's reporting
obligations is the admonition that these duties must be
viewed in the context of the relevant disclosure category to
which Dr. Black is assigned under the Conflict of Interest
Code promulgated,and adopted by the Tahoe-Truckee Unified
School District.2/ Only sources of income falling within
the relevant definition of "reportable income" need be disclosed by Dr. Black. Under the District Code disclosure for
a school director, such as Dr. Black, is less comprehensive

___________________________
1/ All statutory references are to the Government
Code unless otherwise noted.

2/ We have reviewed the provisions of the Code of
the District and note that only those investments in business
entities and sources of income specified in the attached
materials are defined as reportable. It is our view that
the specificity found in the Code will obviate the need for
most of the reporting alluded to in your letter.

Mr. Douglas A. Lewis
December 27, 1977
Page Two


than that required of certain public officials such as members
of Boards of Supervisors or City Councils under Section
87200. With this important caveat in mind, and assuming
that some, but not all, of Dr. Black's "sources of income"
would be reportable under the provisions of the Code, we now
proceed to answer your questions.

Because Dr. Black receives income from his medical
practice, his practice is a source of income to him within
the meaning of Sections 82030 and 87103(c). In addition,
Section 82030 defines "income" in relevant part, to include:

.... a pro rata share of any income of any business
entity or trust in which the individual ... owns
.... a 10-percent interest or greater.

Thus, along with Dr. Black's medical practice
itself, certain patients of Dr. Black's who provide income
to his business entity - the medical practice - will be a
source of income to him. However, as you indicate in your
letter only those sources of income to a business entity
that pay in excess of $10,000 in fees during the applicable
reporting period need be disclosed on a Statement of Economic
Interests. This conclusion is dictated by Section 87207(b)(3)
which provides in relevant part:

(b) When income of a business entity, includ-
ing income of a sole proprietorship, is required
to be reported under this article, the statement
shall contain:

...

(3) In the case of a business entity
not covered by paragraph (2), the name of
every person from whom the business entity
received payments if the filer's pro rata
share of gross receipts from such person was
equal to or greater than ten thousand dollars
($10,000) during a calendar year.

With respect to your third question, it is our
conclusion that the patient, not the patient's insurance
carrier, is the source of income to Dr. Black. The policy

3/ The Conflict of Interest Code of the Tahoe-
Truckee Unified School District contains language similar to
the cited provisions.

Mr. Douglas A. Lewis
December 27, 1977
Page Three


underlying disclosure of certain sources of income under the
Act and Conflict of Interest Codes adopted pursuant thereto
is stated in Section 81002(d). That section provides:

     (d) Assets and income of public officials
which may be materially affected by their official
actions should be disclosed and in appropriate
circumstances the officials should be disqualified
from acting in order that conflicts of interest
may be avoided;

     We believe this policy envisions the existence of
potential conflicts of interest in those cases in which
certain economic relationships may interfere with a public
official's exercise of objective judgment in making govern-
mental decisions. In this case, most decisions to enter
into an economic relationship with the doctor would be made
by the patient, not the insurance carrier. Since the decision
to enter into the relationship is made by the patient and
not the insurance company, it is not likely that the payment
by the insurance company on behalf of the patient will in-
fluence the doctor's government decisions which may affect
the insurance company.

     Accordingly, Dr. Black should report the names of
those patients otherwise reportable under the Code who,
along with their insurance companies, paid Dr. Black fees in
excess of $10,000 during the reporting period. The exception
to this general rule would be if the insurer, or some other
person or business entity sent patients to Dr. Black pursuant
to some formal agreement or plan. Under such circumstances,
we might reach a different conclusion, but it is our under-
standing that this is not the case in the instant situation.

     We turn now to your final question which asks
whether Dr. Black in light of Sections 87100 and 87103 1/
(and the disqualification provisions of the School District's

1/ Those sections provide that:

     87100. No public official at any level of state
or local government shall make, participate in
making or in any way attempt to use his official
position to influence a governmental decision in
which he knows or has reason to know he has a
financial interest.

                                        (continued next page)

Mr. Douglas A. Lewis
December 27, 1977
Page Four


Conflict of Interest Code) must disqualify himself from
participation in decisions of the Board relating to health
and welfare benefits received by employees of the District
if one or more of the employees of the District are also his
patients.

     As indicated, above, under the terms of the Act,
both Dr. Black's medical practice and certain patients of
that practice will be "sources of income" to him within the
meaning of Section 87103(c), supra. Thus, it is necessary
to analyze the effect of the Board's decisions concerning health
and welfare benefits of employees will have on those various
sources of income. As indicated above, the insurer of
Dr. Black's patients generally are not sources of income to
him. Therefore, the analysis must center on the effect of
the decision on his own practice (or his patients), not the
effect of the decision on his patient's insurance carriers.

     There are two ways in which the Board's decision
regarding health benefits might possibly affect Dr. Black's
patients and practice. First, you have suggested that an
insurance company might give Dr. Black "preferential treatment" if he selected or recommended a particular policy or
plan for the employees of the District and that such "preferential treatment" will have a foreseeable material financial
effect on his practice. In the absence of specific facts,
we think this hypothesis is so speculative as to not be a
"foreseeable" consequence of the decision within the meaning
of Section 87103.

___________________________
(footnote 4 continued)

     87103. An official has a financial interest in a
decision within the meaning of Section 87100 if it
is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will
have a material financial effect, distinguishable
from its effect on the public generally, on:

     (c) Any source of income, other than loans
by a commercial lending institution in the regular
course of business, aggregating two hundred fifty
dollars ($250) or more in value received by or
promised to the public official within twelve
months prior to the time when the decision is
made; ...

Mr. Douglas A. Lewis
December 27, 1977
Page Five


     Second, you suggested that Dr. Black's decisions
on health and welfare plans may affect his practice by in-
creasing or decreasing the number of patients who use his
services. The fact that Dr. Black specializes in bone frac-
tures leads us to a conclusion that the majority of his
decisions concerning health and welfare benefits will not
have a foreseeable material financial effect on either his
practice or those patients who constitute sources of income
to him. It is unlikely that any person would choose to
cease or begin using Dr. Black's services merely because of
his decisions concerning the health plan. We also does not
appear probable that the decision of the District would
involve either including or excluding the health plan's
coverage of bone fractures. Even if he did make a decision
concerning bone fracture coverage there is no evidence to
suggest that the decision would materially affect Dr. Black's
practice.

     We would be remiss if in the context of this advice
we did not raise one more potential issue not specifically
addressed in your letter. Since some employees of the District
are sources of income to Dr. Black, the question might arise
as to whether Dr. Black may participate in decisions concerning
matters such as employee wages, terms and conditions of
employment, disciplinary actions, etc. We do not think that
the Act should be read as prohibiting Dr. Black from partici-
pating in this wide range of Board actions. Support for
this conclusion is garnered from the Commission's regulation
defining "material financial effect," 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section
18702, a copy of which is enclosed. In view, given the
financial relationship of Dr. Black to his patients, we do
not believe that in matters such as these the existence of
the financial interest will interfere with Dr. Black's per-
formance of his duties in an impartial manner free from
bias. Accordingly, we conclude that he probably will not be
prohibited from participating in the bulk of matters coming
before the District Board.

___________________________

     5/ Although there is no suggestion in this case
that health plan decisions will affect the doctor's practice,
there may be cases where a decision to increase the scope of
health plan coverage would lead to a substantial increase in
a doctor's business. Such a situation might arise if, for
example, it was foreseeable that the decision of a psychiatrist-
school board member to extend a health plan to include psy-
chiatric treatment would result in an increase in the number
of persons seeking such treatment.

Mr. Douglas A. Lewis
December 27, 1977
Page Six


Thank you again for writing. I trust this letter
answers your questions. If you wish to appeal the denial of
your opinion request, you may do so pursuant to 2 Cal. Adm.
Code Section 18321. If we can be of further assistance in
this or any other matter, please feel free to contact Kenneth
Goshorn of the Conflicts of Interest Division at (916) 322-
6444.

Sincerely,

[signature]

Michael Bennett
Executive Director

MB:KWG:TP:plh
Enclosure

DOUGLAS A. LEWIS
PLACER COUNTY SCHOOL ATTORNEY
1234 HIGH STREET
AUBURN, CALIFORNIA 95603
(916) 823-1714

October 28, 1977

Fair Political Practices Commission
P. O. Box 807
Sacramento, California 95804

Gentlemen:

     Pursuant to Government Code § 3311, I am requesting an
advisory opinion on behalf of Dr. James Black, a member of the
Board of Trustees of the Tahoe Truckee Unified School District.

     The questions posed are as follows:

     1. In reporting Dr. Black's income (other than gifts)
from his solo medical practice, is he required to list individual
clients or insurance companies who may pay him individual fees of
$250.00 or more, or may he report his income in his medical
practice as "Dr. James Black, medical practice, address, and check
the appropriate box"?

     2. In reporting his income from his medical practice as
"Income of Business Entities Which do not Provide Legal or Brokerage
Services", is he required to list the individual names of patients
and/or insurance companies who pay his fees of $10,000 or more?

     3. If the answer to question No. 2 is yes, and a portion
of his fee is received from an insurance company, is he required to
report the income as being received from the insurance company or
from the individual patients or both? (Assume, that the total
received from a single insurance company is more than $10,000 and
covers more than one patient.)

     4. Should Dr. Black disqualify himself from participating
in decisions of the governing board relating to health and welfare
benefits received by employees of the Tahoe Truckee Unified School
District, if one or more of the employees of the district are also
his patients?

Fair Political Practices Commission
Page 2
October 28, 1977

The foregoing questions assume that there is a material
foreseeable effect on his income.

The facts are as follows:

1. Dr. Black is a sole private medical practitioner
specializing in bone fractures.

2. He receives income from a medical practice from
individuals as well as insurance companies.

3. There are many employees of the Tahoe Truckee Unified
School District that are also patients of Dr. Black.

4. The Tahoe Truckee Unified School District is served in
isolated and there is an extremely limited number of physicians and
doctors available and Tahoe Truckee Unified School District is one
of the largest employers in the area with about 650 employees.

5. That the Board of Trustees of the Tahoe Truckee Unified
School District provides health and welfare benefits to its employees
which include insurance for medical care and treatment.

My understanding of the law that Dr. Black would report
his income from his medical practice (assuming personal living as a
sole proprietorship and that he would simply list the source of his
income "his medical practice" and check the appropriate box. In
the event that he receives more than $10,000 from a single source,
he would also be required to disclose on Schedule C - "Income of
Business Entities which do not Provide Legal or Brokerage Services"
that the disclosure could list the insurance company if the aggregate
amount received was more than $10,000 and he would not report the
name of patient as a source of income unless the patient actually
paid in $10,000 or more in addition to the amount received from the
insurance company.

The question as to whether or not Dr. Black should disqualify himself from participating in the health and welfare benefits
of the employees of the Tahoe Truckee Unified School District, if
they are his patients, I think is a closer one since it is conceivable that an insurance company could give Dr. Black preferential

Fair Political Practices Commission
Page 3
October 28, 1977


treatment if he selects and/or recommends a particular health
and welfare policy or plan for the employer at the basic level.
It would, therefore, be my conclusion that it would be best for
Dr. Black to refrain from participating in the selection of any
particular health and welfare benefit that would be provided
to employees of the Tahoe Truckee Unified School District.

If you need any further information, please let me
know.

Very truly yours,


DOUGLAS A. LEWIS

DAL/vc

embedding.first_500_words: State of California Fair Political Practices Commission P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814 Technical Assistance/Administration • Executive/Legal • Enforcement • Conflict of Interest (916) 322 5560 322 5901 322 6441 322 6444 December 27, 1977 Mr. Douglas A. Lewis 77-12-087 Placer County Schools' Attorney 1230 High Street Auburn, CA 95603 Re: Opinion Request No. 77-038 Dear Mr. Lewis: Thank you for your letter to the Commission dated October 28, 1977, requesting an opinion concerning certain disclosure and disqualification responsibilities of a member of the Tahoe-Truckee Unified School District. The Commission will not issue a formal opinion concerning the matters in question because your request does not raise substantial issues of interpretation under the Political Reform Act ("Act"). See 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18329(b). However, I trust the following informal advice issued pursuant to Government Code Section 83114(b)1/ will enable you and Dr. Black to resolve the matter in question. Initially we discuss Dr. Black's reporting obligations, as set out in questions one through three of your letter. Paramount to any discussion of Dr. Black's reporting obligations is the admonition that these duties must be viewed in the context of the relevant disclosure category to which Dr. Black is assigned under the Conflict of Interest Code promulgated,and adopted by the Tahoe-Truckee Unified School District.2/ Only sources of income falling within the relevant definition of "reportable income" need be disclosed by Dr. Black. Under the District Code disclosure for a school director, such as Dr. Black, is less comprehensive ___________________________ 1/ All statutory references are to the Government Code unless otherwise noted. 2/ We have reviewed the provisions of the Code of the District and note that only those investments in business entities and sources of income specified in the attached materials are defined as reportable. It is our view that the specificity found in the Code will obviate the need for most of the reporting alluded to in your letter. Mr. Douglas A. Lewis December 27, 1977 Page Two than that required of certain public officials such as members of Boards of Supervisors or City Councils under Section 87200. With this important caveat in mind, and assuming that some, but not all, of Dr. Black's "sources of income" would be reportable under the provisions of the Code, we now proceed to answer your questions. Because Dr. Black receives income from his medical practice, his practice is a source of income to him within the meaning of Sections 82030 and 87103(c). In addition, Section 82030 defines "income" in relevant part, to include: .... a pro rata share of any income of any business entity or trust in which the individual ... owns .... a 10-percent interest or greater. Thus, along with Dr. Black's medical practice itself, certain patients of Dr. Black's who provide income to his business entity - the medical practice - will be a source of income to him. However, as you indicate in your letter only those sources of
